questions.prompt: Your agent inferred 'user prefers Python' from one ambiguous message. Two months later every session opens with Python-flavored examples — but the user actually prefers Go. What's the fix?

choices.text: Delete all memory and start over.

Tag every stored fact with confidence and source (user-stated, user-confirmed inference, agent inference, behavioral pattern). Auto-expire low-confidence inferences. Add a 'review my memory' UI so users can correct stored facts. The original problem was treating an inference as a canonical fact.

questions.explanation: Memory poisoning happens when low-confidence inferences become canonical facts. The fix is a trust hierarchy: explicit statements > user-confirmed inferences > agent inferences > behavioral patterns. Higher trust = longer retention; lower trust = expire and require re-confirmation.

questions.prompt: What MUST you eval in a long-term memory system?

choices.text: Only recall — did the agent remember the fact?

Recall (remembered facts), boundedness (didn't pull irrelevant past context), update (used new value when fact changed), AND cross-user isolation (no leaks between users). Cross-user leaks are major privacy incidents — explicitly eval for them.

questions.explanation: Memory has multiple failure modes. Eval each one: recall (does it remember?), boundedness (does it avoid irrelevant noise?), update (does it use current values?), cross-user isolation (does it stay scoped?). Skip any and you're shipping a memory bug class blind.
